title: Counter-Height Bar Stool
summary: A backed bar stool from 2x2 legs and 1x3 rails, with a slatted 1x4 seat — a sturdy, stackable-looking stool you can build several of in a weekend.
description:
A backed bar stool at counter height, built from cheap dimensional lumber and pocket screws. The taller back legs run all the way up to form the backrest, and a ladder of 1x3 rails between them gives the back its look and its rigidity. It's a repeatable build — make one to dial in the process, then knock out a matching set.

The geometry is all in the rail placement, so be consistent. On the front legs the bottom rail sits 4-1/2" up; on the back legs the first rail is 12" up with the back rails spaced 2" apart. And when you join the front and back with the side rails, keep the top rails consistent on all four sides — all flush to the outside edge, or all set in — or the seat won't sit flat and square.

The seat is five 1x4 slats screwed down from underneath through upward-facing pocket holes, so no fasteners show on top. Place the frame upside down on the seat slats, center it with an even gap all around, and fix it. Sand the joints well to knock down any pocket-hole proud spots and clean off glue squeeze-out before finishing — on a seat, every rough spot gets found.
timeLabel: 4–6 hrs per stool, less for a batch

tools.note: All joinery, plus upward holes for the seat.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut two front legs at 24", two back legs at 45", two bottom rails, front/back at 14-1/2" and two bottom rails, sides at 15-1/2" from the 2x2. Cut one seat trim, back frame at 14-1/2" from the 1x2. Cut six rails, front and back at 14-1/2" and two top rails, sides at 15-1/2" from the 1x3. Cut five seat slats at 18-1/2" from the 1x4. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to three 8 ft 2x2, one 6 ft 1x2, two 6 ft 1x3 and one 8 ft 1x4. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build the front leg frame
steps.body: Join the two 24" front legs with one 14-1/2" 1x3 and a 14-1/2" 2x2, placing the bottom edge of the 2x2 rail 4-1/2" up from the bottom of the legs. Drill the pocket holes for the rails and fasten with glue and screws.

steps.title: Build the back leg frame
steps.body: Join the two 45" back legs with four 14-1/2" 1x3s and a 14-1/2" 2x2, placing the 2x2 rail 12" up from the bottom and spacing the 1x3 back rails 2" apart. This ladder forms the backrest.

steps.title: Join front to back
steps.body: Connect the front and back frames with a 15-1/2" 1x3 and a 15-1/2" 2x2 on each side. Keep the top rails consistent on all four sides — all flush to the outside edge or all set in — so the seat sits flat.

steps.title: Add the back seat rail
steps.body: Add the back 1x3 rail with its top flush with the two side rails and flush to the front of the leg. Drill upward-facing pocket holes on the inside and fix the 14-1/2" 1x2 seat trim to it, ready to grab the seat.

steps.title: Attach the seat and finish
steps.body: Drill upward pocket holes on the inside of the frame. Place the five 1x4 seat slats good-side-down and set the upturned stool frame on them, centered with an even gap all around, then fasten from underneath. Sand the joints well and remove glue residue, then stain or paint.

images.alt: Counter-height bar stool with a slatted seat and a ladder back
